entry.title: Joule in ABAP: KI trifft Entwicklung
entry.content:
ABAP entwickelt sich weiter – und diesmal geht es nicht nur um neue Features.
Künstliche Intelligenz hält Einzug in unseren Entwickleralltag.

Mit Joule bringt SAP generative AI direkt in die Entwicklung.
Die Frage ist längst nicht mehr OB, sondern WIE SCHNELL sich unser Arbeiten verändern wird.

In der Live-Session
👉 Joule in ABAP
bekommt ihr einen kompakten Einblick, was das konkret bedeutet.- Durchgeführt von meinem Kollegen Jonathan Schwarz.

🚀 Was sich verändert?

  • Code entsteht nicht mehr nur manuell
  • Logik wird schneller verstanden
  • Routineaufgaben werden automatisiert

💡 Warum das relevant ist?

Joule ist mehr als ein Tool – es verändert, wie wir entwickeln:
👉 weniger Tippen, mehr Denken
👉 weniger Suchen, mehr Fokus

🎯 Fazit

Die nächste Entwicklungsstufe ist greifbar nah.

👉 Die Zukunft klopft an.
Bist du dabei?
https://learning.sap.com/live-sessions/joule-in-abap

entry.title: From Strategy Doc to Runtime Rule: Embedding SAP AI Standards into Claude Code
entry.content:
What if your AI coding assistant already knew every relevant product standard before you typed a single line?

Disclaimer

This is a personal exploration, not an official SAP tool or recommendation. It is grounded in SAP's published standards and shaped by real development work, but the ANAR framework, the Claude Code integration, the hooks, and the skills described here are not SAP products and are not an endorsed approach. This is an experiment in what becomes possible when published strategy and engineering standards are treated as machine-readable development instructions rather than as PDFs on a SharePoint. Where I refer to SAP standards or tool availability, readers should verify applicability through their own official SAP channels before using anything like this in a productive context.

 

The Gap Between Strategy and Code

SAP has done serious work defining what "AI-native" can mean for BTP products. There is the AI-Native North Star Architecture, the AI Golden Path, and a growing body of product standards and development guidelines that together describe many of the requirements for a production-grade AI-native BTP application.

The problem I kept running into: these documents live in different repositories. My code lives in a CAP project. There is a wide gap between reading a standard and having it influence the code I am actually writing on a Tuesday afternoon.

The usual answer to this gap is a review checklist: someone runs an assessment at the end of a sprint or before a release, flags what is missing, and the team scrambles to retrofit it. I have done this. It is slow, expensive, and it catches things too late.

I have been calling this assessment lens ANAR - AI-Nativeness and Agent-Readiness. It is a framework I have been developing, based on SAP's official product standards and north star architecture, for assessing how ready a BTP product actually is for AI-native operation. It has ten weighted domains: Strategic Vision, UX & Joule, Process & Agents, Data & Grounding, API & Discoverability, Agent Tooling, AI Runtime, Security & Governance, Platform & Ops, and Commercialization. It also has twelve hard gates that cap the maximum score if they fail.

The maturity bands:

ScoreBand
85-100AI-Native
70-84Agent-Ready
55-69AI-Ready
35-54Pilot
0-34Not AI-Ready

The point of the bands is to make the next step concrete. A product at Pilot band might have two specific things blocking AI-Ready: G4, no direct LLM imports, and G8, data protection. Those are two engineering tasks, not a vague "improve your AI maturity".

But the more interesting question is not how to score your product. It is how to stop needing the score as a retrofit:

What if the standards were not something you checked against, but something that was already part of the environment you code in?

 

The Idea: Standards as Constitution

Claude Code - an AI coding assistant available in my development context - supports project memory through CLAUDE.md. It is a Markdown file that sits at the root of your project and is loaded as persistent project context. Whatever you put in it can strongly shape the assistant's behaviour, but it is not a policy engine. For hard enforcement, I used hooks.

That was the insight. A product standard regarding AI agent security might haves a set of requirements: objective declaration, constraints, tool permissions, Human-in-the-loop (HITL) policy, emergency stop endpoint. Those are not only audit criteria. They can also be expressed as coding rules. Some rules belong in the assistant's project context. The nonnegotiables need enforcement.

So I wrote CLAUDE.md as an ANAR constitution: twelve standards-aware sections, each mapping directly to a SAP product standard, written as coding rules rather than compliance prose.

## Audit Logging — G10 
Every state-changing handler MUST call audit.log() with:
  actor_type ('agent'|'human'), actor_id, object_type, object_id,
  tenant, x-correlation-id.
console.log() is NOT a substitute.

Not "please consider logging". A standing rule in the project context. The constitution gives the assistant the rules; the hooks make the nonnegotiables enforceable.

 

The Framework: Four Layers

Layer 1 - Foundation (always-on)

CLAUDE.md keeps the core rules visible in every session and gives the assistant the context it needs to generate standards-aware code. Alongside it, a set of memory files load the reference knowledge the assistant needs: all 12 gate definitions, canonical code patterns per gate, 15 anti-patterns with grep signatures, and a SAP service to ANAR domain map.

Layer 2 - Interception (real-time)

Claude Code supports hooks - shell scripts that fire before and after tool calls. This is where guidance becomes guardrail. I wrote four:

Hook

Trigger

What it does

gate-guard.js

Before every Write/Edit

Blocks direct LLM imports (G4), warns on unprotected CDS actions (G9), warns on missing audit.log() (G10)

anar-lint.sh

After every Write/Edit

Dispatches file-type-specific lint: CDS files check @restrict and ORD annotations; agent YAML checks security fields; mta.yaml checks service bindings

deploy-gate.js

Before any deploy command

Reads the ANAR scorecard and blocks deployment according to the configured gate policy

session-delta.sh

End of session

Shows ANAR-relevant changes, current score, and the highest-value next action

 

The gate-guard hook is the one that changed my workflow most noticeably. The first time it blocked a require('xyzai') import with:

[ANAR/G4] Direct LLM import - use permitted ai-api

I realised what ambient standards feedback actually feels like.

Layer 3 - Skills (on-demand)

Claude Code skills can be invoked directly with slash commands and work like specialized instructions the assistant executes on request. I built twelve. Here are the most useful:

  • /anar-scaffold <Entity> - generates a complete ANAR-compliant CAP service: schema.cds with @ODM.entityName and @PersonalData annotations, service.cds with @restrict on every action, service.js with audit logging and actor_type detection, plus mta.yaml additions
  • /anar-agent <AgentName> - scaffolds a code-based agent with all seven safeguards in the definition YAML
  • /anar-genai-call <UseCase> - generates the correct SAP AI Core SDK call pattern, never a direct third-party import
  • /anar-gate [G1-G12] - checks a specific gate against the current codebase with evidence and exact remediation steps
  • /anar-review - full 10-domain assessment, scores 0-5 per domain, updates the scorecard JSON, reports the delta
  • /anar-deploy-check [env] - pre-deployment checklist that distinguishes dev, staging, and prod strictness

Layer 4 - Intelligence (agents + MCP)

Two agent definitions can run as autonomous subagents: a Gate Guardian that monitors gate compliance and posts structured findings, and an ANAR Coach that explains requirements, generates examples, and runs gap analyses. Plus an MCP server exposes five tools for live standard lookups, callable by other agents in multi-agent workflows.

 

The Lifecycle in Practice

The way this plays out in a session:

While coding: CLAUDE.md means scaffolded output tends to include @ODM.entityName, @restrict, audit.log(), and actor_type detection from the start. The gate-guard hook catches anything important that slips through.

End of sprint: /anar-review scores the current state, updates the scorecard, and tells you the three highest-value things to do next, ranked by domain weight multiplied by score gap. Not a generic "improve security", but something like "Add emergency_stop_endpoint to order-agent/definition.yaml (G7, +4 pts)."

Before deployment: deploy-gate.js fires automatically and applies the configured gate policy. In my framework, prod mode requires all twelve gates to pass. Staging blocks on the six critical security gates. Dev mode is advisory only.

Before commerzialisation: /anar-commercialize walks through the commercialization checklist, checks for feature_id in agent definitions, and verifies metering hooks are wired. /anar-release-gate ai-native produces a local readiness statement that can be attached to a release PR as supporting evidence, not as a replacement for official governance.

The anar-scorecard.json is the living state: committed alongside the code, updated by /anar-review after each sprint, with a sprint log of score progression over time. It is the audit trail.

 

What This Is Not

This is not a product. It is not a supported SAP tool. It is not even a finished framework.

It is an experiment in a specific hypothesis: that the gap between "we have good standards" and "our code follows them" is primarily a tooling gap, not a knowledge gap. Engineers generally know what they should do. The question is whether the default path - the path of least resistance in the IDE - produces standards-aware code or not.

CLAUDE.md + hooks makes the standards-aware path the path of least resistance. That is the only claim I am actually making.

It also does not replace official review, approval, security, data-protection, or release-governance processes. At best, it moves many obvious issues earlier, where they are cheaper to fix.

The ANAR framework itself - the 10 domains, the 12 gates, the scoring - is my interpretation of SAP's published standards. I have tried to be rigorous about grounding every rule in a specific product standard, but interpretations can be wrong, standards evolve, and my coverage is certainly incomplete. If you are doing this for a real product heading toward production, run the actual SAP AI self-assessment process. Use the official tools. This is for experimenting, learning, and building intuition about what AI-native BTP development actually requires.

 

Building Your Own

The pattern does not require any specific repo. For a real SAP product, I would treat this as a local developer-experience layer, not as a replacement for official assessments, architecture reviews, security review, data-protection review, or release governance.

The minimal version is three things.

1. A project memory file that translates standards into rules.

Pick the product standards that apply to your product. For each one, write a concrete coding rule - not "ensure authorization is applied" but "every CDS action and function must have @restrict or @requires. No exceptions." The more specific the rule, the more reliably the assistant can follow it.

2. A PreToolUse hook that enforces the non-negotiables.

One Node.js script, called before every file write, can grep-match for the patterns you absolutely cannot ship: direct LLM provider imports, unprotected CDS actions, or state-change handlers without an audit call. Anything that should be a hard block goes here.

3. A scorecard JSON committed to the repo.

One file records the current domain scores, gate statuses, and sprint log. It does not need to be automated at first. Even a manually updated JSON makes the current state visible, trackable over time, and readable by any agent or script that needs to reason about it.

Those three pieces substantially narrow the gap between "we have a standard" and "our code follows it" - not by eliminating judgment, but by making the standards-aware path the path of least resistance. Everything else - the skills, the agents, the MCP server - builds on top of that foundation.

The authoritative sources to ground your own rules in:

  • SAP AI-Native North Star Architecture - domain definitions and maturity model
  • SAP's AI Golden Path - approved technology stack and agent patterns
  • SAP BTP Developer's Guide - platform conventions and approved libraries

These are official SAP resources. Ask your product standard owner or check your own internal developer portal.

 

What Is Next

The experiment I am running next: making the scorecard itself an agent-readable artifact, so a planning agent can read the current band and open backlog, then generate a prioritized engineering plan to reach the next one. If that works, the gap between assessment and sprint planning closes too.

I would be interested to hear how others are turning architecture standards, security requirements, or AI governance rules into developer-time feedback loops rather than end-of-sprint review checklists.

entry.title: Upcoming Migration Webinars: Accelerate your transition from PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
If you're still running your integrations on SAP PI/PO, you already know what's coming. On-premise integration has had its day - and the window to act is now!

To help you move forward with confidence, we're hosting a two migration webinars dedicated to one thing: getting you from PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite without the guesswork.

Why now?

SAP PI/PO served its purpose. But the integration landscape has changed. Modern enterprise architecture demands cloud-native flexibility, real-time connectivity, and the ability to scale fast - across SAP and non-SAP systems alike. SAP Integration Suite delivers exactly that: a single, unified platform that consolidates your entire integration landscape and opens the door to AI-powered automation. Staying on PI/PO isn't standing still. It's falling behind.

What you'll learn:

SAP PI/PO Migration — The Big Picture
Where does your migration journey start, and where does it lead? We'll set the stage with a concise overview of the move from PI/PO and what success looks like.

Assessment Tool - See It in Action
Get introduced to the migration assessment capability and watch a live demo of its key features. Know where you stand before you make a single move.

Migration Tool - How It Actually Works
A practical walkthrough of the migration tooling - what it does, how it supports your transition, and what to expect when you use it.

What's New in the Community
Latest resources, documentation updates, and community contributions worth knowing about. Stay current without having to dig for it.

What's Coming Next
A snapshot of the migration roadmap - upcoming milestones, priorities, and planned enhancements. So you can plan ahead, not just react.

Q&A
Open floor. Bring your questions, edge cases, and real-world scenarios. We'll tackle them live.

 

How to join?

Interested? Great! Then make sure you register for one of following migration Webinars via the links below:

 

  • Session in English: 14th of July 2026  [Register here]
  • Session in Chinese: 16th of July 2026 [Register here]

 

See you there!

entry.title: SAP Developer News for June 25th, 2026
entry.content:
DESCRIPTION

Upcoming Events

  • We are Developers
  • CodeConnect

SAP Open Source Webinar on the NeoNephos Foundation

  • Webinar details https://events.sap.com/ospo-webinar-series/en_us/home.html

ABAPConf  Recordings

  • YouTube Recordings

DYK GenAI in SAP Build Process Automation

  • More information: https://community.sap.com/t5/application-development-and-automation-blog-posts/create-sap-build-apps-applications-in-minutes-with-ai/ba-p/14054862

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro

0:10 Upcoming Events

1:40 SAP Open Source Webinar on the NeoNephos Foundation

2:38 ABAPConf Recordings

3:45 DYK GenAI in SAP Build Process Automation

Transcription

 

00;00;00;00 - 00;00;06;00
Announcer
This is the SAP Developer News for June the 25th, 2026.

00;00;06;02 - 00;00;09;24
Thomas Jung
the Developer Advocates are always on the move and evidently almost never

00;00;09;26 - 00;00;26;23
Thomas Jung
at home. Over the next couple of weeks, besides our normal schedule of CodeJam, we got a couple of special events coming up and just a little over a week many of us are going to be at. We are Developers in Berlin. Now, this isn’t an SAP.

00;00;26;25 - 00;00;53;24
Thomas Jung
This is not a specific SAP event, but one just a general event for developers from all around the world. I'll be there. Witalij and Shilpa and Sheena will also be there. So if you happen to find yourself at this event, stop by, say hi, maybe attend one of our sessions. I'm giving a session on Friday about how AI is affecting Developer Relations, Developer Advocacy.

00;00;53;24 - 00;01;25;04
Thomas Jung
And maybe I'll tell you my story about how an AI coding agent decided it was its role to lie to me. It should be a fun story. But then the week after that, many of us will be at CodeConnect and near the SAP main campus. Over in St Leon/ROT, we have HANACon, UI5Con and reCAP, as well as some CodeJams that week, and a great many of the Developer Advocates will be in town to support those events.

00;01;25;06 - 00;01;41;18
Thomas Jung
Speak at them, run the CodeJams, so we look forward to seeing many of you at CodeConnect. So busy couple of weeks as we kick off the summertime in the Northern Hemisphere here, and we hope to see you at an event soon.

00;01;41;21 - 00;02;15;21
DJ Adams
Coming up next month on Friday the 10th of July, there's another SAP Open Source webinar. It's open to all. And the topic is the Neo Foundation Europe's Cloud Sovereignty in Motion. The foundation was established in 2025 as a Linux Foundation Europe initiative, and its core objectives are to strengthen European tech sovereignty, improve interoperability standards, and to enable integrated cloud to edge solutions.

00;02;15;23 - 00;02;34;25
DJ Adams
In this webinar hosted by SAP's Open source Program Office, you can learn how the foundation has evolved, how it's governed, and what it offers, a link to more details, and how and where to register is in the description.

00;02;34;27 - 00;03;06;05
Thomas Jung
This week we saw the ABAPConf online and in-person hybrid event take place, and if you miss it, there are plenty of recordings available on YouTube. In fact, both live streams from the whole day. Track one. Track two are available on YouTube, so something like nearly 18 hours worth of content 18 hours plus we saw some great sessions there by some of our ABAP PM and development colleagues.

00;03;06;08 - 00;03;37;21
Thomas Jung
You know, kicking off the day, talking about the future of ABAP Cloud, about the RAP development model, about ABAP Development Tools now available in Visual Studio Code, and what we're doing with AI tooling there. And at the end of the day, we saw a special presentation by Karl Kessler as he looked back at the history of ABAP. And so all that great content is available on the ABAPConf page on YouTube.

00;03;37;21 - 00;03;46;22
Thomas Jung
And if you're interested in ABAP definitely suggest you check it out.

00;03;46;25 - 00;04;18;06
Rekha D R
Did you know you can enable and use generative AI feature with an SAP Build Process Automation? For that, click on Automated Process Project, provide the name and create the project. Then create a process within it. Switch to the overview page, then click on generate button. Now you have the options to create process, form and data type. When you open the process and click generate button, you can explain this process.

00;04;18;09 - 00;04;36;06
Rekha D R
You can create a business form. For example, a sales order form and AI will intelligently generate the entire form with relevant input fields within the decision artifact. You can summarize the decision. Also have the option to generate new rules with your natural language.

entry.title: Two Decades of Proven Excellence: Syntax Achieves SAP Golden Certificate While Driving Innovation
entry.content:
How do you keep mission-critical SAP environments running flawlessly while continuously pushing innovation forward? In this interview, Syntax shares how its deep SAP heritage shapes a modern, global approach to operations – and marks a major milestone along the way. By achieving SAP certification for the 10th consecutive time, Syntax has earned the prestigious Golden Certificate, underlining its long-standing commitment to quality and operational excellence. Combined with newly achieved global certifications in SAP Security Operations and SAP Business AI Operations, this recognition highlights Syntax's ability to deliver secure, future-ready services at scale. From cloud transformation to the growing role of SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and AI, discover how Syntax helps customers turn SAP operations into a true driver of business value.

Could you start by telling us about your company and the services you offer?

Syntax is a global technology solutions and services provider with deep expertise in running and transforming mission-critical enterprise environments. We help customers manage complexity across cloud, applications, infrastructure, security, and end-user services, with SAP being one of our core strategic focus areas.

In the SAP space, we support customers across the full lifecycle: from advisory, migration, and implementation through to technical managed services, application management, optimization, and innovation. That includes operating highly business-critical SAP landscapes, supporting transformation to SAP S/4HANA and cloud-based environments, and helping customers unlock additional value through SAP BTP, automation, security, and AI.

What differentiates Syntax is our Boutique @ Scale approach. The ability to combine strong operational discipline with a forward-looking innovation mindset, tailoring every engagement to our customers' unique priorities. Customers come to us not only to keep their SAP environments stable and secure, but to evolve them as business needs change, backed by the agility and personal care of a boutique, delivered with the reach and resilience of a global enterprise.

 

 

Your SAP roots run deep, brought to Syntax through Freudenberg IT, whose SAP pedigree reaches back almost to SAP's founding. How has that heritage shaped who Syntax is today as an SAP operations partner – and is it still something you draw on?

Our heritage through Freudenberg IT has had a lasting impact on who we are as an SAP operations partner. It shaped a culture centered on reliability, accountability, process discipline, and long-term customer commitment, all of which remain essential when you are operating business-critical SAP environments.

That background gave us a very practical understanding of what enterprise customers need from an operations partner: not just technical expertise, but consistency, governance, responsiveness, and the ability to support transformation without compromising stability.

We absolutely still draw on that heritage today. While Syntax has evolved into a global organization with a broad portfolio and a modern cloud- and innovation-focused vision, the core DNA remains the same: trusted operations, deep SAP knowledge, and a commitment to helping customers run mission-critical systems with confidence.

 

Looking back at those early years of the partnership, what inspired your decision to seek your first service certification in 2002 – initially in Germany – and what then drove the decision to expand that certification globally in 2006?

From the beginning, we saw certification not simply as a badge, but as an important way to benchmark ourselves against SAP's own operational standards and to validate the quality of the services we were delivering to customers. Starting in Germany made sense because that was where our SAP operations heritage was strongest and where we had built deep experience in supporting complex customer landscapes. As customer expectations grew and our delivery model became increasingly international, expanding that certification globally was the next natural step. It reflected two priorities: first, giving customers the confidence that they would receive the same high-quality service wherever they operated; and second, creating a globally consistent operational framework across our teams. In that sense, certification supported both trust and scale.

Handover of the golden certificate, June 2026

 

How would you describe Syntax's SAP operations proposition to a customer who doesn't want to choose between operational reliability and continuous innovation?

Our message would be very simple: you should not have to choose between the two. At Syntax, we believe the strongest SAP operations model is one that protects the business today while enabling transformation for tomorrow. That means delivering stable, secure, and highly governed day-to-day operations while also helping customers adopt new capabilities in areas such as cloud, automation, SAP BTP, and AI.

We often describe this as helping customers optimize the "run" so they can invest more confidently in the "grow". In practice, that means reducing operational risk, increasing visibility, improving efficiency, and creating the right foundation for continuous innovation. For customers, the outcome is not just better uptime; it is a more agile SAP environment that supports long-term business change.

 

 

How do you experience the audits in practice, and what role do they play for your organization?

We see the audits as rigorous, constructive, and valuable. They are an important discipline for the organization because they require us to demonstrate - not just describe -  the maturity of our processes, controls, and service delivery practices.

In practice, audits help us maintain a strong level of operational consistency across teams and regions. They also create a useful feedback loop, highlighting where we are strong and where we can continue to improve. Internally, that reinforces accountability and continuous improvement; externally, it gives customers and SAP additional confidence in the quality and reliability of our services.

So for us, audits are not just compliance exercises. They are an important part of how we sustain operational excellence at scale.

 

For the first time, you achieved global certification for SAP security operations and SAP Business AI operations - can you tell us how your competencies around Security and Business AI operations are adding value to your service offerings?

These certifications are important because they reflect the evolving customer expectations. Today, customers want innovation, but they also want it delivered in a way that is secure, governed, and operationally robust. On the security side, our capabilities help customers strengthen the protection of their SAP landscapes through a combination of foundational controls, continuous monitoring, vulnerability management, privileged access governance, and broader security operations support. That adds value by reducing risk, improving visibility, and helping customers operate with greater confidence in increasingly complex hybrid environments.

On the Business AI side, the value is about helping customers move from interest to operational reality. It is one thing to explore AI use cases; it is another to run them reliably in enterprise environments. Our role is to help customers embed AI into their SAP landscape in a scalable, supportable way that is aligned with operational best practices. Together, Security and Business AI operations allow us to help customers innovate more safely and more effectively.

 

As SAP is strongly focusing on the cloud and encourages partners to support customers in their migration journey, how are you already helping customers in this area - and looking three to five years ahead, what role do SAP BTP and AI play in Syntax's vision and how do you expect your capabilities to evolve?

We are already supporting customers across the full cloud migration journey: from assessment and planning through migration, conversion, upgrade, and post-go-live operations. That includes helping customers modernize existing SAP environments, move to cloud-based operating models, and build the governance and support structures required for long-term success.

A major part of our value is that we do not view migration as a one-time project. We see it as part of a broader transformation of our operating model. Customers need a partner that can help them move, stabilize, optimize, and then continue to innovate.

Looking three to five years ahead, we see SAP BTP playing a central role as the platform for clean-core extensions, integration, automation, and data-driven innovation. We also expect AI to become much more deeply embedded - both in business processes and in the way SAP environments are operated and optimized. For Syntax, that means continuing to evolve our capabilities in platform operations, cloud governance, automation, observability, security, and AI-enabled service delivery. Our vision is to help customers build SAP operating models that are not only cloud-ready, but also innovation-ready.

 

If you could give one piece of advice to a customer who is just beginning to think about their SAP operations strategy for the next decade, what would it be?

If we had to highlight one key recommendation, it would be this: treat SAP operations as a strategic business capability rather than just a technical support function.

Over the next decade, organizations will need to balance resilience, security, cost efficiency, and continuous innovation far more closely than before. Those that succeed will be the ones who design their operating models early on, with standardization, automation, cloud readiness, and strong governance built in from the outset.

Ultimately, it is important not to separate your operations strategy from your transformation strategy. Reliable and well-governed operations are not just the foundation for stability - they are what make sustainable innovation possible.

 

Information on the certification program for operations partners, as well as an overview of all certified partners, can be found in the SAP Operations Partner Guide.

Visit OPC at SAP.com or on the SAP Partner Portal.

entry.title: SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 1: Event-Driven Architecture, Topics, Queues and Initial Setup
entry.content:
SAP Event Mesh — Part 1: Event-Driven Architecture & Setup

A 3-part hands-on series on SAP Event Mesh: Event-Driven Architecture, Pub-Sub, and CPI integration.

Series at a glance
Part 1 (this post):  EDA concepts + provisioning the Event Mesh instance, queues, subscriptions, and service key

Part 2: Testing with Postman + CPI iFlow configuration (publish & consume)
Part 3:  Common errors, fixes, and a quick-reference cheat sheet

Environment used in this series: SAP BTP·

Most SAP integration developers learn point-to-point patterns first RFC, SOAP, OData. They work, but they tightly couple systems together. The moment you need a *second* system to react to the same business event, you end up duplicating calls and adding brittle dependencies.

SAP Event Mesh flips that model. In this first post we'll cover the architectural concepts you need, then walk through provisioning Event Mesh end to end so it's ready for the integration work in Part 2.

1. Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — Core Concepts

1.1 What is Event-Driven Architecture?

Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) is a software design pattern in which systems communicate by producing and consuming events. Instead of calling each other directly (RFC, SOAP, OData), systems publish events to a central hub, and any interested system subscribes to those events.

Why EDA over traditional integration:

- Loose coupling — systems don't need to know about each other
- Scalability — multiple subscribers can consume the same event
- Resilience — if a subscriber is down, events wait safely in the queue
- Real-time processing — events are handled as they happen

1.2 How Event Mesh works — the Pub-Sub flow

   

 

 

 



Flow explained 

1. S/4HANA Cloud (the publisher) sends an event to a topic (e.g. `CustomerMaster`).
2. Event Mesh receives the event and routes it to every queue subscribed to that topic.
3. Each subscriber system (Shopify, C4C, 3PL) has its own queue.
4. Consumer systems pull messages from their own queue, independently of each other.

The key insight: one publish can fan out to many consumers, and each consumer reads at its own pace.

1.3 The three types of events

Event Type Description SAP Example

Notification EventA lightweight event that notifies consumers that something has happened. It typically contains minimal information and prompts consumers to retrieve additional data if required.A Business Partner is created in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, triggering a notification event.
Data EventAn event that carries the actual business data, allowing consumers to process the information without making an additional API call.A Customer Master event containing customer details such as name, address, and contact information.
Transaction EventAn event representing the completion of an entire business transaction, often including related business objects and line-item details.A Sales Order Confirmation event containing the order header, customer information, and all line items.



1.4 Key terminology

Term Description Example

TopicA logical communication channel where publishers send events. Every queue subscribed to a topic receives a copy of the published event. Topics typically follow a namespace-based naming convention.S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/CustomerMaster
QueueA durable message store dedicated to a specific consumer. Each consuming application (such as Shopify or SAP Sales Cloud) should have its own queue to process messages independently.S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify
NamespaceA hierarchical prefix used to organize and manage topics and queues. It ensures consistent naming and controls which resources can publish or subscribe to events.S4HC/Testing/Masterdata
PublisherThe application or service that generates and publishes events to a topic. Publishers do not need to know who consumes the events.SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), Custom Applications
Subscriber (Consumer)The application or service that receives events by reading messages from its subscribed queue. Each consumer processes messages independently.Shopify, SAP Sales Cloud, 3PL System
Pub-Sub ModelA messaging pattern where publishers send events to topics, and subscribers receive them through their own queues without direct communication between systems. This enables loose coupling and scalability.One Customer Master event consumed by multiple downstream systems
SAP Event MeshSAP's managed messaging service that routes events from publishers to subscribed queues using the Publish-Subscribe architecture. It provides reliable asynchronous communication between applications.Routes a Customer Master event from SAP S/4HANA Cloud to Shopify, SAP Sales Cloud, and a 3PL system
EMIS (Event Mesh Integration Suite)The embedded SAP Event Mesh service available within SAP Integration Suite. It provides the same messaging capabilities as standalone SAP Event Mesh but is managed directly through Integration Suite.Event Mesh accessed from SAP Integration Suite

 

2. SAP Event Mesh – Initial Setup

In this section, we'll provision an SAP Event Mesh instance, create queues, configure queue subscriptions, and generate a service key. By the end of these steps, your Event Mesh environment will be ready for publishing and consuming events in Part 2 of this series.

💡EMIS vs. Standalone SAP Event Mesh

Event Mesh Integration Suite (EMIS) is the embedded Event Mesh service available within SAP Integration Suite. It provides the same core messaging capabilities as the standalone SAP Event Mesh service, including topics, queues, subscriptions, and asynchronous message routing.

The primary difference is how you access and manage the service:

  • Standalone SAP Event Mesh is provisioned and managed directly from the SAP BTP Cockpit.

  • EMIS is accessed and managed through the SAP Integration Suite application.

For the concepts covered in this series—such as topics, queues, publishers, subscribers, and service keys—the configuration and behavior are essentially the same regardless of which deployment option you use.

Step 1 – Create an SAP Event Mesh Instance

The first step is to provision an SAP Event Mesh instance in your SAP BTP subaccount. During instance creation, you'll define the namespace, messaging capabilities, and authorization rules that govern how publishers and subscribers interact with Event Mesh.

Follow these steps:

  1. Sign in to the SAP BTP Cockpit and navigate to your Subaccount.

  2. From the left navigation pane, select Services → Service Marketplace.

  3. Search for Event Mesh and select the service.

  4. Click Create (or Create Instance, depending on your BTP version).

  5. Choose the appropriate Service Plan.

  6. In the Service Instance Parameters section, paste the following service descriptor JSON.

  7. Click Create to provision the Event Mesh instance.

Service Descriptor

// {
  "resources": { "units": "10" },
  "options": { "management": true, "messagingrest": true, "messaging": true },
  "rules": {
    "topicRules": {
      "publishFilter":   ["${namespace}/*"],
      "subscribeFilter": ["${namespace}/*"]
    },
    "queueRules": {
      "publishFilter":   ["${namespace}/*"],
      "subscribeFilter": ["${namespace}/*"]
    }
  },
  "version": "1.1.0",
  "emname": "S4_RudhTesting",
  "namespace": "S4HC/Testing/Masterdata"
}

💡Tip

The namespace acts as the root path for all topics and queues created within your Event Mesh instance. For example, if your namespace is:

S4HC/Testing/Masterdata

then every topic and queue should begin with this prefix, ensuring a consistent naming convention and simplifying access control.


Step 2 — Create queues

Each consuming system needs its own queue. In the Event Mesh dashboard:

1. Open the Event Mesh dashboard .

 


2. Go to the  Queues tab.
3. Click **Create Queue** for each consumer:
   - `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify` — for Shopify integration
   - `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/C4C` — for C4C / SAP Sales Cloud
   - `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/3pl` — for the 3PL / logistics system

💡 Best Practice

Queue names should always begin with your configured namespace to maintain a consistent naming convention and simplify resource management.

Each consuming application should have its own dedicated queue. When an event is published to a topic, SAP Event Mesh delivers a copy of the message to every queue subscribed to that topic. This allows each consumer to process the event independently, without affecting other subscribers.

 

Step 3 – Subscribe Queues to Topics

After creating the queues, the next step is to subscribe each queue to the topic(s) from which it should receive messages.

Follow these steps:

  1. Open the SAP Event Mesh Dashboard.

  2. Navigate to the Queues tab and select the required queue.

  3. Click the Subscriptions (or Queue Subscriptions) icon.

  4. Choose Add Subscription and enter the topic name.

  5. Save the subscription.

Example

Queue Subscribed Topic

S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/ShopifyS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify/CustomerMaster
S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/C4CS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/C4C/CustomerMaster
S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/3PLS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/3PL/CustomerMaster

⚠️ Important

Topic names are case-sensitive. For example, CustomerMaster and customermaster are treated as two different topics.

Ensure that the topic name configured by the publisher exactly matches the topic subscribed to by the queue. Even a small difference in letter casing or spelling will prevent messages from being delivered.

💡 Best Practice

Establish a consistent topic naming convention early in your project and reuse it across SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), and SAP Event Mesh. Consistent naming reduces configuration errors and makes troubleshooting much easier.

Step 4 – Create a Service Key

A Service Key contains the connection details and authentication credentials required for external applications and integration flows to connect securely to SAP Event Mesh.

The generated JSON includes information such as the OAuth 2.0 client credentials, token endpoint, messaging endpoints, and management API URLs that are used by applications like SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), Postman, or custom applications.

Follow these steps to create a Service Key:

  1. In the SAP BTP Cockpit, navigate to your Event Mesh service instance.

  2. Open the Service Keys section.

  3. Click Create Service Key.

  4. Enter a meaningful name for the service key (for example, EventMesh_CPI_Key).

  5. Click Create.

  6. Once the service key has been generated, open it and download or copy the JSON. This JSON contains all the credentials required to authenticate and connect to your Event Mesh instance.

Service Key – Important Fields

Field Purpose

namespaceDefines the namespace prefix used for topics and queues.
management.uriREST endpoint for managing queues, subscriptions, and other Event Mesh resources.
management.oauth2.tokenendpointOAuth 2.0 token endpoint used to obtain access tokens for the Management API.
messaging[httprest].uriREST endpoint used to publish messages over HTTP.
messaging[amqp10ws].uriAMQP over WebSocket endpoint used by applications such as SAP Cloud Integration.
uaa.clientidOAuth 2.0 Client ID used for authentication.
uaa.clientsecretOAuth 2.0 Client Secret used to obtain access tokens.

🔒 Security Best Practice

Treat the Service Key as a sensitive credential. Never publish or share your Client ID, Client Secret, tenant-specific OAuth endpoints, or downloaded Service Key JSON in public repositories, blogs, or documentation.

If a Service Key is accidentally exposed, regenerate the credentials immediately and update all consuming applications.

What's Next?

Congratulations! You now have a fully configured SAP Event Mesh environment, including:

  • An SAP Event Mesh instance

  • A configured namespace

  • Queues for each consumer

  • Queue subscriptions

  • A Service Key for secure authentication

Your environment is now ready for end-to-end event integration.

In Part 2 of this series, we'll move from configuration to implementation by validating the setup with Postman and building a complete integration using SAP Cloud Integration (CPI). We'll cover:

  • Publishing messages to SAP Event Mesh using an HTTP iFlow

  • Consuming messages from SAP Event Mesh using an AMQP iFlow

  • Verifying end-to-end message flow between publishers and consumers

In Part 3, we'll explore common configuration issues, troubleshooting techniques, best practices, and a quick-reference cheat sheet based on real-world implementation experience.

I hope this article helped you build a solid foundation in SAP Event Mesh and Event-Driven Architecture. If you found it useful, feel free to share your feedback or questions in the comments.

Happy Learning! 🚀

Tags

SAP Event Management SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry runtime and environment SAP Integration Suite SAP Cloud for Customer integration with SAP CRM  SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

entry.title: Demystifying Model Context Protocol (MCP), Part 3: Integration with Joule
entry.content:
Recap and Final Steps

In Part 2, we made the concepts concrete by building a commerce MCP server using FastMCP, exposing agent‑ready tools that wrap Commerce (CAP‑based) OData APIs. You saw how to run the server locally, configure destinations and deploy the MCP server on the cloud. By the end of Part 2, you had a fully running commerce MCP server, with tools like get_products and get_retailers discoverable and testable via an MCP inspector.

In this final Part 3, we will move from a standalone MCP server to a true end‑to‑end agentic experience by integrating it with an MCP client in SAP Joule. Building on the same commerce MCP server, we will use Joule Studio to:

  1. Create a content based Joule Assistant
  2. Understand Prompt Engineering
  3. Configure the Commerce MCP server (Built in Part 2)
  4. Test and release the Joule Assistant

By the end of this series, you will have an end to end working model for an enterprise use case to design, build an MCP server and use it in Joule Agent.

Pre-requisites:

  1. You should have access to SAP Build
  2. You should have access to joule studio
  3. You should be able to configure destination in destination service

Let's get started.

Architecture and Building Blocks

The following solution diagram illustrates the main components of the application from a agent creator / developer perspective.

The main components of the above diagram are explained below

  1. The central entry point for building custom Joule skills and AI agents created is Joule Studio. Joule Studio is part of SAP Build, which includes SAP Build Apps, SAP Build Code, and SAP Build Process Automation. It leverages SAP Build's user experience and lifecycle management capabilities.
  2. Joule Studio enables extending Joule by creating new capabilities that can be deployed alongside the out-of-the-box capabilities provided by SAP. These capabilities include Joule Skills and AI Agents. 
  3. AI Core plays an important role in extending Joule's capabilities. It provides the underlying Large Language Models (LLMs) that can be leveraged to configure AI Agents. 
  4. SAP BTP Connectivity integrates AI Agents with SAP and non-SAP systems. This gives access to business data which is important to provide context to the LLMs.
  5. Once developers have created their custom Joule Skills and AI Agents, they can use SAP Build's lifecycle capabilities to compile and deploy these capabilities. This grants business users access to additional functionalities, helping them in their daily tasks through centralized access via Joule, reducing the need to switch between multiple SAP and non-SAP applications.
  6. Joule Studio relies on SAP Cloud Identity Services for identity management, authentication and identity life-cycle management. SAP Cloud Identity Services act as a central facade for identity and access management, offering secure authentication or federation with third-party identity providers. 

Hands-on

Step-1: Configure Destination For Remote MCP Server

The first thing Joule needs is a BTP destination that points to your deployed commerce MCP server. Joule Studio discovers MCP servers exclusively through HTTP destinations that are marked as MCP-capable.

  1. In the SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to your Joule Studio subaccount, then go to Connectivity - Destinations - New Destination.
  2. Create an HTTP destination that points to the root URL of your commerce MCP server (for example, the Cloud Foundry route of your FastMCP app). Do not append /mcp to the URL here; Joule Studio will add the MCP path later when you configure the server in the agent.
  3. Choose an appropriate Authentication type for your MCP server (for example, NoAuthentication for a simple lab setup, or OAuth2ClientCredentials if your MCP server is protected by XSUAA). Fill the client ID, client secret, and token service URL from the corresponding service key of your XSUAA instance when using OAuth.
  4. Add the mandatory additional property so Joule can recognize this destination as an MCP server:
    1. Property: sap-joule-studio-mcp-server
    2. Value: true
  5. Save the destination and click Check Connection to verify that BTP can reach your MCP server endpoint.

Step-2: Joule Studio Project Set Up

With the BTP destination in place, the next step is to set up a Joule Studio project and agent that will later consume the commerce MCP server.

Pre-requisites:

  1. From the SAP Build lobby, open the Control Tower tile.
  2. Go to the Environments section and create the environment where you plan to run your Joule agent later.
  3. Next, Go to Destination section and Add new destination. You should see the destination in the dropdown list which you configured in step-1. This is because of the property: sap-joule-studio-mcp-server.

Create Joule Studio Project:

  1. From the SAP Build lobby, create a new project of type - Joule Agent and Skill
  2. Add Name: "Commerce AI Project"and Description: "This project consists of e-commerce AI assistant agents"
  3. Once the project is created, Open the project. Inside it, create "Joule Agent". Give Name, Identifier and Description. Refer screenshot below 

Step-3: Prompt Engineering

For Step 3, you can focus on how to “teach” the Joule agent to use the commerce MCP tools reliably.

Now that the Joule project and environment are ready, the next crucial step is to engineer the agent’s brain through its instructions. In Joule Studio, this lives in the Expertise & Instructions section and acts as the system prompt that guides how the agent reasons, when it calls MCP tools, and how it responds back to the user. I am giving the example below

  • Expertise: 
You are a commerce assistant that helps business users discover products, check availability, and find retailers using the commerce MCP tools available to you.​
  • Instructions: 
PURPOSE AND OBJECTIVE:
The E-Commerce AI Assistant is designed to assist the user with any e-commerce related questions using the configured MCP tools.

PROCESS:
1. Analyze the user's query to determine the specific information or action requested (e.g., categories, products, products by category, or retailers).
2. Invoke only the relevant tools that matches the user's request. For example:
   - If the user asks for categories, invoke the tool to retrieve categories only.
   - If the user requests products, invoke the tool to retrieve the product list.
   - If the user requests products by category, first invoke the tool to get the categories. Then from the category response, get the relevant category id to fetch the products related to the category.
   - If the user requests retailers, invoke the tool to retrieve retailer information.
3. Do not fetch additional data if not asked by the user. If the user asks product information, do not get any additional information about retailers or orders etc. 
4. Fetch only the data which is required to provide answer to the user.
5. Format and present the output in a clear and structured manner for the user.
6. If any required information is missing or an error occurs, ask the user for clarification or report the issue clearly.

TOOLING:
- Use the configured MCP server to fetch tools to retrieve relevant data from the MCP server.
- Ensure all communication is clear and concise, and handle errors gracefully.​
  • Additional Context:
Please maintain a clear, professional, and supportive tone. This agent is designed to assist e-commerce users and operations teams in understanding available products and categories. Also provide retailers who can fulfill the products which the user is interested in.

Please hide informations like - ID, tax id or SSN from the response.

Recommendations should be practical, action-oriented, and phrased respectfully, especially when issues are detected. The agent must avoid vague language. If data is unavailable, it should state so explicitly and guide the user accordingly. 

The overall voice should reflect operational reliability, transparency, and collaboration, aligning with values of efficiency, accountability, and continuous improvement.

Next is to provide the LLM model settings of your choice. I have used Open AI models for planning and response. You can configure Agent Execution Steps based on your preference.

Step-4: Configuring MCP Server

  1. Click Add MCP Server.
  2. In the dialog, provide a Name such as Commerce MCP Server so that it is clear which backend domain this server represents.
  3. For BTP Destination, select the HTTP destination you created earlier (for example, COMMERCE_MCP_DESTINATION) that already has sap-joule-studio-mcp-server=true.
  4. Once you select the destination, you will see the list of tools exposed by the MCP server.
  5. Click Add Server.
  6. Confirm and save. 

Step-5: Test and Release the Joule Assistant

Once the MCP server is configured in Joule Studio, the final step is to validate the assistant in a private environment and then release it for use. This is where you confirm that the agent can interpret prompts correctly, call the commerce MCP tools, and return grounded responses instead of generic answers.

During testing, check three things carefully:
  1. The agent chooses the right MCP tool for the user intent.
  2. The tool response is summarized clearly and accurately in natural language.
  3. Missing or ambiguous inputs trigger a clarifying question instead of a wrong tool call or a hallucinated answer.
If the behavior is not what you expect, go back to the prompt instructions and refine the agent’s goals, tool guidance, or response style. In practice, prompt tuning is usually an iterative step, a small wording change can significantly improve the overall output.
 
After the testing looks solid, release the agent to the private environment so it can run as a usable Joule assistant. Once you click Release, Go back to Control Tower - Joule Assistants. In the Standalone Assistants section, you will see your custom joule assistant. Open the joule assistant and start the conversation. 

 

What we built so far ?

By the end of this blog series, we built a Joule assistant for commerce scenarios that is powered by a remote MCP server. The setup connects a BTP destination, a private runtime environment, and a Joule Studio Agent so the assistant can discover and call commerce tools instead of answering generically.

The end result is an end-to-end flow where a user prompt goes into Joule, Joule uses the MCP tools exposed by your server, and the assistant returns grounded commerce data such as products, categories, and retailers.
 
In other words, you turned a standalone MCP server into a usable enterprise AI assistant inside Joule Studio.

Disclaimer: This is not an official reference application or documentation. The thoughts outlined in this blog are based on my real world experience and learnings.

Feel free to “like“, “Share“, “Add a Comment” and to get more updates about my next blogs follow me!

References

  1. https://architecture.learning.sap.com/docs/ref-arch/06ff6062dc/3
  2. https://help.sap.com/docs/Joule_Studio/45f9d2b8914b4f0ba731570ff9a85313/193461e9d8494995bab4889b22afac93.html
